content.text: Every React application produces two distinct tree structures during a render: the
(the hierarchical model of React components) and the

(the actual HTML nodes in the browser). Understanding the difference between them clarifies why React components can compose freely without imposing structure on the DOM, and how React knows which parts of the UI to update.

attrs.alt: React render tree vs DOM tree side by side
attrs.title: Custom components exist only in the render tree; host elements become real DOM nodes

content.text: The render tree is React's internal model of a UI — a tree of
linked by parent-child relationships. Each node in the render tree corresponds to one
— not a DOM element. The root of the tree is whatever component you pass to
This tree is React's internal data structure (implemented as a linked list of "fiber" nodes). It does not appear in the browser's DOM inspector — it is only visible through React DevTools.
React distinguishes two kinds of nodes in the render tree:
Always leaves of the render tree

Only host components produce actual DOM nodes. When React commits, it recursively expands custom components until only host components remain, then creates the corresponding DOM elements. A custom component like
is entirely erased from the DOM — only what it returns (e.g.,
Viewing the Render Tree in DevTools
The React DevTools browser extension shows the render tree as a component hierarchy in the "Components" tab. This is distinct from the browser's "Elements" tab, which shows only the DOM tree.
— shows the full render tree, including all custom components, their props, state, and hooks. Click any node to inspect it.
— records which components in the render tree re-rendered during a capture, their render times, and why they rendered.
How the Render Tree Changes Over Time
The render tree is not static. Its shape changes on every render as conditional branches appear or disappear, lists grow and shrink, and lazy-loaded components mount.
is added to the render tree and React mounts it (initial render of that subtree). When it flips back,
is removed and unmounted, destroying any state it held.
React's reconciler compares the new render tree to the previous one to compute the minimal set of DOM changes. This comparison works by walking both trees simultaneously:
of the new element to the previous element.
If the type is the same, update the existing instance (preserve state, update props).
If the type changed, destroy the old instance (and all its descendants) and create a new one.
In any render tree, the leaves are the components that render no other custom components — they return only host elements (or
). These are typically the most frequently re-rendered nodes because they are the deepest in the tree and receive the most targeted state updates.
A leaf component re-renders whenever its parent re-renders with the same or different props. If the leaf is expensive, wrap it with
to skip re-renders when its props have not changed.
Render Tree vs Module Dependency Tree
React's documentation distinguishes the render tree from the
statements). Bundlers like Vite and webpack use the module dependency tree to build the final JavaScript bundle; React uses the render tree at runtime to manage component lifecycles.
A page can have multiple React roots — each with its own independent render tree. Components within one root cannot access state in another without external coordination (e.g., a shared store).
React Portals are different: they render a subtree into a different DOM node, but
keep it in the same render tree

. A modal rendered via a portal is still a child of the component that created it in the render tree — it receives context, events bubble through it, and state is shared — even though in the DOM it sits under
Internally, React implements the render tree as a
— a linked list of "fiber" nodes. Each fiber stores a component's type, props, state, effect list, and links to its parent, first child, and next sibling. This structure lets React:
Pause and resume work in the middle of a tree traversal (concurrent mode)
Keep two fiber trees simultaneously — "current" (on-screen) and "work in progress" (being rendered)
Discard the work-in-progress tree if a higher-priority update arrives, without corrupting the current tree
You never interact with fibers directly — they are an implementation detail. What matters to you is the component hierarchy, which is what React DevTools shows.
Understanding the render tree suggests several design principles:
Lift state as high as necessary, but no higher.
State placed near the root triggers re-renders down the entire tree. State at a leaf affects only that leaf.
Switching types at the same position destroys state. Keep types stable; use props to change behavior.
React renders depth-first and the overhead per component is low. Premature flattening (avoiding component composition) is usually wrong.
Host components are the output, not the model.
Design component hierarchies around your data and behavior, not around the shape of the HTML you want.
Common Mistake: Defining Components Inside Render

Defining a component function inside another component's render output creates a new function reference on every render. React sees a different type at that position on every render and unmounts/remounts the child, destroying its state and causing flickering.

When components conditionally render subtrees, the shape of the render tree changes. React tracks position within the tree, so a component at position 0 in a list and position 0 in a different branch are treated as the same node — their state is shared. This is why position matters as much as type.
How Lists Affect the Render Tree

adds multiple children at the same level in the render tree. Each item maps to a position determined by its index (without a key) or by its key (with one). Keys let React maintain stable identity for list items even when the list is reordered.
React Context propagates values down the render tree. A

placed anywhere in the tree makes its value available to all descendants — regardless of how deeply nested they are. This is why context is called a "teleportation" mechanism: it bypasses the props chain.
When the Provider's value changes, React schedules re-renders for every consumer in the render tree — skipping intermediate components that do not use the context, even if they wrap the consumers.
Suspense Boundaries in the Render Tree

wraps a subtree that may be waiting for data or lazy-loaded code. When a component in the subtree suspends (throws a Promise), React walks up the render tree to find the nearest Suspense boundary and shows its fallback instead.

Multiple Suspense boundaries at different levels of the render tree let you show different fallbacks for different parts of the page — a placeholder for the sidebar, a spinner for the feed, and a skeleton for the header can all be independent.
Error Boundaries in the Render Tree

Error boundaries work the same way as Suspense: when a render-phase error is thrown anywhere in the render tree, React walks up to the nearest error boundary component and renders its fallback. Components below the boundary are unmounted.
Error boundaries must be class components — there is currently no hook equivalent. You can write your own, use react-error-boundary, or use framework-level error boundaries (Next.js error.tsx).

A common misconception is that a deep render tree is slow. In practice, React's traversal is O(n) in the number of components and each component function call is cheap. The commit phase (DOM mutations) is usually the performance bottleneck, not tree depth.
The factors that actually affect performance:
— expensive computation inside a component's render function.
— a parent re-rendering causes all descendants to re-render by default.
— large diffs between old and new trees produce more DOM operations.
— effects that fetch data or subscribe on every re-render can be costly.

Flatten the render tree only if you can demonstrate with the Profiler that a specific deep subtree is the bottleneck. Premature flattening sacrifices readability and composability for no measurable gain.
The render tree is React's internal model of component instances — distinct from both the DOM tree and the module dependency graph.
Custom components exist only in the render tree; host components (
The render tree's shape changes on every render as conditional branches and list items appear or disappear.
React reconciles the new render tree against the previous one to compute minimal DOM mutations. Same type at the same position: update. Different type: destroy and recreate.
Portals move DOM nodes but keep components in the same render tree. Multiple
Never define component functions inside another component's render — it creates a new type on every render, causing unnecessary unmounts.
